text: Recreational runners using hard-cushioned shoes have a 52% higher injury risk compared to those using soft-cushioned shoes.
Lighter recreational runners have an 80% higher injury risk when running in hard-cushioned shoes compared to soft-cushioned shoes.
population: lighter recreational runners (below median body mass)

text: Heavier recreational runners do not show a statistically significant difference in injury risk between hard and soft shoe cushioning.
population: heavier recreational runners (above median body mass)

text: Body mass alone is not associated with running injury risk in recreational runners.
The common belief that heavier runners need more cushioning to prevent injuries is not supported; the protective effect of soft cushioning is found only in lighter runners.
Shoe cushioning stiffness values of approximately 61 N/mm (soft) versus 95 N/mm (hard) represent a meaningful difference in injury protection for lighter runners.
